explanation: The self-test procedure is represented by 'SelfTestMode'. The confirmation that local alarms are functional is represented by 'testAlarmsPass'. The property states that globally, whenever the system is in 'SelfTestMode', it must eventually lead to 'testAlarmsPass'. This ensures that the self-test, when active, will always successfully confirm the functionality of local alarms.

The LTL property `G (SelfTestMode -> F testAlarmsPass)` translates the natural language by stating that globally, whenever the system enters or is in `SelfTestMode` (the self-test procedure), it must eventually (`F`) confirm the functionality of local alarms, indicated by `testAlarmsPass`. This captures the 'shall confirm' aspect as a procedural guarantee: the self-test mode will eventually lead to the successful testing of alarms.

The property states that globally (G), if the system is in 'SelfTestMode', then it must eventually (F) be the case that 'testAlarmsPass' occurs. This directly translates "The self-test procedure shall confirm the local alarms are functional" by ensuring that if the self-test is active, the test for local alarms will eventually succeed.

The natural language statement "The self-test procedure shall confirm the local alarms are functional" implies that whenever the system is in `SelfTestMode` (the self-test procedure), it must eventually lead to the successful passing of the local alarms test (`testAlarmsPass`). The LTL operator 'U' (Until) is most appropriate here because it means that `SelfTestMode` must hold continuously until `testAlarmsPass` becomes true, and `testAlarmsPass` must eventually become true. This accurately captures the notion of a procedure running until a confirmation is achieved. If `SelfTestMode` were to end without `testAlarmsPass` occurring, the property would be violated, thus ensuring the confirmation aspect of the self-test.

The natural language statement "The self-test procedure shall confirm the local alarms are functional" implies a guarantee that if the system enters the SelfTest Mode, it must eventually lead to the successful confirmation of the local alarms. The LTL formula G(SelfTestMode -> F testAlarmsPass) captures this by stating that Globally (G), if the system is in SelfTestMode, then Eventually (F) the local alarms test must pass (testAlarmsPass). This ensures that the self-test procedure fulfills its duty to confirm alarm functionality.

The natural language statement "The self-test procedure shall confirm the local alarms are functional" implies a guarantee that if the system is undergoing a self-test, the local alarms must eventually be confirmed as functional. The atomic proposition "SelfTestMode" signifies that the self-test procedure is active. The atomic proposition "testAlarmsPass" indicates that the local alarms test passes successfully, confirming their functionality. The LTL formula `G (SelfTestMode -> F testAlarmsPass)` translates this by stating that 'Globally (G), if the system is in SelfTestMode, then it must Eventually (F) be the case that testAlarmsPass is true'. This ensures that every time the self-test procedure is active, it will lead to the confirmation of local alarm functionality.

The natural language statement "The self-test procedure shall confirm the local alarms are functional" indicates a liveness property. It means that whenever the self-test procedure is active (represented by `SelfTestMode`), it must eventually lead to the successful confirmation of the local alarms being functional (represented by `testAlarmsPass`). The `G` (globally) operator ensures that this condition holds true at all times: for every instance of `SelfTestMode`, `testAlarmsPass` must eventually occur. The `F` (eventually) operator captures the fact that the confirmation might not be instantaneous but will happen at some future point during or after the self-test.

The natural language statement implies that if the system is in SelfTest Mode, it is required to eventually confirm that the local alarms are functional. The atomic proposition 'SelfTestMode' indicates when the self-test procedure is active. The atomic proposition 'testAlarmsPass' indicates that the local alarms test has passed, which signifies they are functional. Therefore, the LTL property states that globally, whenever the system is in 'SelfTestMode', it must eventually reach a state where 'testAlarmsPass' is true, thereby confirming the functionality of the local alarms.

The LTL property "G (SelfTestMode -> F testAlarmsPass)" expresses that globally, if the system is in `SelfTestMode` (i.e., the self-test procedure is active), then it must eventually lead to the `testAlarmsPass` state. This directly captures the requirement that the self-test procedure "shall confirm" the local alarms are functional, implying a successful outcome for the alarm test as a result of the self-test. The `F` (eventually) operator ensures that `testAlarmsPass` will occur at some point after `SelfTestMode` becomes true, fulfilling the confirmation aspect.

The self-test procedure is represented by the atomic proposition "SelfTestMode". The requirement that it "shall confirm the local alarms are functional" means that if the system enters the self-test mode, it must eventually ensure that the local alarms test passes. This is captured by the atomic proposition "testAlarmsPass". The LTL formula states that globally (G), if "SelfTestMode" is true, then eventually (F) "testAlarmsPass" must become true. This accurately reflects that the self-test procedure, whenever initiated, has the goal of confirming alarm functionality, which is achieved by the alarm test passing.
